title: Ink mixing and testing for calligraphy
subtitle: How to dilute, combine, and assess calligraphy inks before committing to a piece
excerpt: Calligraphy ink from the bottle is rarely at the right consistency for the nib you are using. Too thick and it seizes; too thin and the hairlines flood. A short testing sequence before every session saves the piece.
sourceNotes: Ink consistency, dilution, and testing practice is synthesised from Edward Johnston, Writing and Illuminating and Lettering (1906), and from contemporary calligraphy practice literature. Public-domain source: Internet Archive, archive.org/details/writingilluminat00john.

glossaryTerms.definition: The thickness or flowability of a liquid. For calligraphy, the ink viscosity determines how freely it flows from the reservoir to the nib tip and how it behaves on the paper surface.
A natural plant-derived resin dissolved in water, used as a binder and flow improver in calligraphy and watercolour work. A small addition to thinned ink can improve adhesion and slow dry-time.

content.text: Calligraphy ink fresh from the bottle is a starting point, not a final formulation. The right consistency varies by nib size, paper surface, ambient humidity, and the specific ink. A Mitchell Roundhand 0 needs a thinner ink than a Mitchell 6; a coated paper takes a thinner ink than unsized cartridge. Adjusting ink consistency is not optional; it is part of every session.

Before writing on the good sheet, run a standard test sequence on a piece of the same paper you intend to use. Load the nib and draw a series of thick-to-thin strokes (diagonal strokes with the nib fully and then partially in contact). Then draw a few thin hairlines at the normal writing angle.

Assess four things: whether the thick strokes fill without gaps, whether the hairlines are consistent rather than flooding or skipping, whether the ink dries within a few seconds without feathering, and whether the dried strokes have a clean edge. If any of these fail, adjust before proceeding.

To thin ink, add distilled water a half-drop at a time. Tap water works in most areas, but hard water leaves mineral deposits in the nib. Stir rather than shake. Load the nib and test again. A single half-drop of water makes a measurable difference to a small quantity of ink.
To thicken ink, leave the cap off for 10 to 15 minutes to allow some evaporation. Alternatively, add a tiny amount of
and flow-delay without reducing the colour strength.

Iron-gall ink is the historical standard. It starts grey-brown when wet and darkens to deep brown-black as it oxidises on the paper surface. It is slightly acidic, which means it keys well to sized paper and is resistant to water once dry. Modern formulations are buffered to be less corrosive than historic iron-gall. It performs well on unsized and lightly sized papers.

Sumi ink is a carbon-based ink made from pine soot or lamp black in a gum-arabic binder. It dries to a consistent cool black. It is denser than iron-gall and needs more thinning for small nibs. On sized paper it sits on the surface rather than penetrating, which gives sharp edges but less adhesion.
Walnut ink has a warm brown tone and very low viscosity. It thins easily and suits large-nib practice work. Not waterproof once dry; avoid it for pieces that will be worked over with washes.

Calligraphy gouache (designers gouache thinned to ink consistency with distilled water) is the standard for colour work with pointed-pen scripts. Gouache stays in the nib better than acrylic paint and dries matte. The consistency to aim for is heavy cream; test before each session as gouache dries faster in the nib than carbon inks.

Rinse the nib under cold running water and use a cotton pad to wipe it clean after each session. Do not use hot water; it can warp the nib's steel. For iron-gall ink, clean more thoroughly, iron-gall corrodes if left in the nib between sessions. Store inks in a cool, dark place; light degrades carbon-based inks over time.
